title: Banana bread with walnuts
subtitle: A dense, moist quick loaf for overripe bananas
excerpt: Banana bread is the best use of bananas that are too soft to eat. The riper the fruit, the sweeter and more flavourful the loaf. Toasted walnuts add a crunchy contrast to the close, tender crumb. The recipe takes about 15 minutes to prepare and an hour in the oven.
sourceNotes: Banana bread became a staple of British home baking in the mid-twentieth century, borrowing the American quick-bread tradition of leavening with baking powder and bicarbonate of soda rather than yeast. The combination of overripe banana, butter, sugar, eggs and flour is documented in post-war British cookbooks and has remained unchanged in its essentials. This recipe follows the established British formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf and freeze in a sealed bag for up to 3 months. Toast from frozen or thaw at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The loaf improves overnight as the crust softens and the flavour develops. Store wrapped at room temperature for up to 3 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: Bananas for quick breads must be very ripe, with skins that are more black than yellow and fruit that mashes to a near-liquid paste. Overripe bananas have converted most of their starch to sugar, which is why banana bread is sweet without needing as much added sugar as other cakes. Underripe fruit gives a starchy, bland result.

content.text: The most important ingredient in banana bread is patience: you need to wait until the bananas are properly
. The skins should be almost entirely black. Peel one and mash it; it should be soft enough to press flat with a fork. If the flesh still feels firm or the fruit smells sharp, leave it another day.

Toast the walnuts before adding them. Raw walnuts are bitter and give the loaf a flat, slightly green taste. Spread them on a dry frying pan over medium heat for 4 minutes, shaking regularly, until fragrant.
Heat the oven to 170 degrees C. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line with baking paper. Mash the bananas in a large bowl until smooth.

Stir the 80 g melted butter, 130 g light brown sugar, 2 eggs and 1 tsp vanilla extract into the mashed banana. Sift over the 250 g flour, 1 tsp bicarbonate of soda, half a teaspoon baking powder and half a teaspoon salt, then fold together until just combined. Stir in the 80 g toasted walnuts.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Bake for 55 to 65 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out with just a few moist crumbs, the top is deep brown, and the loaf is firm when pressed gently. If the top browns too quickly, lay a loose piece of foil over it after 40 minutes. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack and leave for at least 20 minutes before cutting.

items.problem: Loaf is gummy or dense in the centre
items.solution: Underbaked or the bananas were too liquid. Very wet bananas can be drained briefly; alternatively, add an extra 5 minutes of baking time and test again with a skewer. The internal temperature should reach 95 degrees C.
The bananas were not ripe enough. The fruit must be heavily spotted or fully black for enough sugar conversion. Very green or firm bananas produce a bland, starchy loaf.
Toss the chopped walnuts in a teaspoon of flour before folding them in. The flour coating helps them stay suspended in the batter during baking.
